title: Abstract
content: Ga 2 O 3 was used as a photocatalyst for the photoreduction of CO 2 in the presence of H 2 as a reductant; in this process CO gas was selectively produced at room temperature and ambient pressure.The optimum conversion of CO 2 was approximately 3 using b-Ga 2 O 3 calcined at 1073 K for 6 h.The formation of CO depended on the amounts of H 2 and CO 2 introduced into the system.The photoreduction of CO 2 on Ga 2 O 3 involves two chemisorbed species-H 2 and CO 2 .This implies that the photocatalytic reaction would proceed on the basis of the typical Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism.

title: Experimental
content: Ga 2 O 3 samples used in this study were synthesized by a conventional reverse-strike method.An aqueous solution of Ga(NO 3 ) 3 (Kojundo Chemical Laboratory Co., Ltd., 99.999) was added to 25 mass NH 3aq to produce Ga(OH) 3 .The precipitated gel of Ga(OH) 3 was filtered under vacuum; washed with distilled water; dried at 353 K; and calcined for 6 h at various temperatures.A Ga 2 O 3 reference sample was prepared by the calcination of commercially available Ga 2 O 3 (Kojundo Chemical Laboratory Co., Ltd., 99.999) at 1273 K for 6 h in a furnace.The crystal structure of each Ga 2 O 3 sample was determined from an X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD), and the specific surface area was calculated from N 2 adsorption using the BET method. The reactants were purified before use in the following manner.H 2 was purified by passing it through a liquefied nitrogen and molecular sieve trap.CO 2 was purified by vacuum distillation at the temperature of liquid nitrogen.The photoreduction of CO 2 was carried out in a closed gas circulation system connected to a vacuum line.Ga 2 O 3 (1.0 g) was placed at the flat bottom of a quartz reactor (dead space: 150 mL).Prior to photoreduction, the catalyst sample was heated and evacuated at 673 K and then treated with O 2 (80 kPa) for 60 min.CO 2 (150 lmol) and H 2 (50 lmol) were introduced into the reactor.The catalyst sample was irradiated from the flat bottom of the reactor with a 200 W Hg-Xe lamp equipped with optical fibers, a collective lens, and a mirror (San-Ei Electric Co., Ltd., UVF-204S Type B).The area subjected to illumination was 19.6 cm 2 .The reaction was analyzed using an online TCD gas chromatograph (Shimadzu Corporation, GC-8A) equipped with a column packed with active carbon and helium (He) as a carrier gas.

title: Results and discussion
content: The crystal structures of a and b phases of Ga 2 O 3 samples calcined at more than 823 K and more than 923 K, respectively, were determined from the X-ray diffraction patterns, as shown in Fig. 1. In addition, we obtained the c phase of Ga 2 O 3 calcined at 773 K using ethyl alcohol as a substitute for H 2 O.The BET specific surface areas of commercially available Ga 2 O 3 and those of the b phase of Ga 2 O 3 calcined at 923, 1073, and 1273 K were 2.7, 19, 17, and 12 m 2 g A1 , respectively.The BET specific surface areas of the a and c phases of Ga 2 O 3 were 20 and 130 m 2 g A1 .Fig. 2 shows the UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra of various phases of the Ga 2 O 3 samples.The diffuse reflectance spectra were converted to absorption spectra on the basis of the Kubelka-Munk theory.The absorption edges were blue-shifted in the order corresponding to the a, b, and c phases.The bandgap energies of the b phases of all the Ga 2 O 3 samples (calcined at 923, 1073, and 1273 K) were almost the same.The bandgap energies were estimated using Davis-Mott's empirical equation, as described below where a is the absorption coefficient; hm the energy of the irradiated light; and E g the bandgap energy.n is an experimental value, and its values for indirect allowed, indirect forbidden, direct allowed, and direct forbidden transitions are 2, 3, 12, and 32, respectively.Using this equation (n 12, direct allowed transition) the bandgap energies of the a, b, and c phases of Ga 2 O 3 (abbreviated to a-, b-and c-Ga 2 O 3 as described below) were estimated to be 5.1, 4.8, and 4.5 eV, respectively. The photoreduction of CO 2 with H 2 as the reductant produces only CO at room temperature, although Yoshida and co-workers 23 have reported that CO 2 was not reduced to CO in the presence of H 2 over Ga 2 O 3 under UV irradiation at 473 K. Fig. 3 shows the amount of CO formed over Ga 2 O 3 calcined at various temperatures in the presence of CO 2 and H 2 under photoirradiation for 5 h at room temperature.In the absence of photoirradiation, CO was not detected in the gas phase.In addition, the reaction did not proceed at 373 K in the dark.The rate of evolutions of CO was initially 0.74 lmol h A1 ; however, it decreased gradually with the photoirradiation time.The amount of CO formed over all the synthesized Ga 2 O 3 samples was higher than that over commercially available Ga 2 O 3 .b-Ga 2 O 3 calcined at 1073 K showed the highest activity (2.0 lmol).The amount of CO formed over b-Ga 2 O 3 calcined at 1073 K was about three times that over commercially available Ga 2 O 3 .This difference in the amounts of CO formed probably due to the fact that the specific surface area of b-Ga 2 O 3 is larger than that of commercially available Ga 2 O 3 .The activity of c-Ga determined from the XRD pattern was very low.These results suggest that the photocatalytic activity is affected by both the crystallinity and specific surface area.It is reported that Ga 2 O 3 chemisorbs H 2 and CO 2 24,25.Fig. 4 shows the adsorption isotherm of H 2 on b-Ga 2 O 3 after the adsorption of CO 2 and evacuation at room temperature.It indicated that the amount of adsorbed H 2 is saturated in 150 lmol of H 2 gas (equilibrium pressure: 2.5 kPa) introduced into this system.Therefore, 50 lmol of introduced H 2 gas (equilibrium pressure: 0.83 kPa, the reaction condition in Fig. 3) is scarcely able to cover all H 2 adsorption site in the presence of adsorbed CO 2 .Fig. 5 shows the dependence of the amount of CO formed over b-Ga 2 O 3 under photoirradiation for 5 h on various amounts of introduced H 2 gas.From the figure, it can be observed that there was an increase in the amount of CO until the introduction of 150 lmol of H 2 gas, thereafter the amount of CO formed was constant, irrespective of the increase in the amount of introduced H 2 gas.The change in photoactivity corresponded to the change in the adsorption isotherm of H 2 on b-Ga 2 O 3 .Accordingly, the amount of CO formed by the photoreduction of CO 2 in the presence of H 2 depends on the amount of introduced H 2 gas.This implies that the adsorption of H 2 on b-Ga 2 O 3 involves the photoreduction of CO 2 .We have reported that basic metal oxides such as ZrO 2 12131417,18 and MgO 15,16 exhibit high photocatalytic activity in the photoreduction of CO 2 .We have suggested the reaction mechanism for this type of photoreduction.In the case of MgO, it was found that the amount of H 2 gas did not affect the photocatalytic activity.The photoreduction over b-Ga 2 O 3 is expected to be different from that over basic metal oxides. On the other hand, CO 2 was also adsorbed on b-Ga 2 O 3 since base sites were present on the surface of Ga 2 O 3 .The adsorption isotherm of CO 2 on b-Ga 2 O 3 indicated that the amount of chemisorbed CO 2 was almost saturated in 100 lmol of CO 2 gas introduced in our system.In fact, the amount of CO formed over irradiated b-Ga 2 O 3 was constant when more than 100 lmol of CO 2 gas was introduced.Therefore, the photoreduction of CO 2 in the presence of H 2 as the reductant on irradiated b-Ga 2 O 3 involves two adsorbed species-H 2 and CO 2 .This implies that the photocatalytic reaction would proceed on the basis of the typical Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism.At present, we assume that b-Ga 2 O 3 has not only the site where CO 2 or H 2 is adsorbed but also the site where both CO 2 and H 2 can be adsorbed.We have found that the adsorption isotherm of CO 2 on b-Ga 2 O 3 after the adsorption of H 2 and that of H 2 on b-Ga 2 O 3 after the adsorption of CO 2 is inconsistent with the conventional adsorption isotherm of CO 2 and H 2 on b-Ga 2 O 3 , respectively.The adsorption behaviors of CO 2 and H 2 and the conformation of the adsorbed CO 2 and H 2 species on b-Ga 2 O 3 are now under investigation. In this study, the optimum conversion of CO 2 was approximately 3; this result was obtained in the case of b-Ga 2 O 3 calcined at 1073 K for 6 h in the saturated amounts of chemisorbed CO 2 and H 2 , which approached in the presence of 100 lmol CO 2 and 150 lmol H 2 .This is one of the best methods for the photoreduction of CO 2 .b-Ga 2 O 3 is one of the promising photocatalyst materials for the photoreduction of CO 2 to CO in the presence of H 2 as the reductant.The adsorption of H 2 on b-Ga 2 O 3 enhanced the photoreduction of CO 2 ; however, this enhancement was not observed in the case of other basic metal oxides such as ZrO 2 and MgO.

title: Conclusions
content: Ga 2 O 3 was used as a photocatalyst for the photoreduction of CO 2 to CO in the presence of H 2 as the reductant at room temperature and ambient pressure.The optimum conversion of CO 2 was approximately 3; this result was obtained in the case of b-Ga 2 O 3 calcined at 1073 K for 6 h in the presence of 100 lmol CO 2 and 150 lmol H 2 .Both H 2 and CO 2 were chemisorbed on the surface of b-Ga 2 O 3 ; therefore, the formation of CO as a result of the photoreduction of CO 2 depended on the amounts of these chemisorbed species. Fig. 1.X-ray diffraction patterns of Ga 2 O 3 calcined at (a) 773, (b) 823, (c) 923, (d) 1073, (e) 1273 K and (f) commercial product (Kojundo Chemical Laboratory Co., Ltd., 99.999):(d) b phase, (N) a phase, and (j) c phase. Fig. 2. UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra of (a) a-, (b) b-and (c) c-Ga 2 O 3 . Fig. 4. Adsorption isotherm of H 2 on b-Ga 2 O 3 after the adsorption of CO 2 and evacuation at room temperature. Fig. 5. Dependence of the amount of CO formed over b-Ga 2 O 3 under photoirradiation on various amounts of introduced H 2 gas.CO 2 : 150 lmol, photoirradiation time: 5 h. K. Teramura et al. Chemical Physics Letters 467 (2008) 191-194 This study was partially supported by the Program for Improvement of Research Environment for Young Researchers from Special Coordination Funds for Promoting Science and Technology (SCF), commissioned by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT) of Japan, and a Grant-in-Aid for Young Scientists (B), 19760542, 2007, from the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS).
